title: ## STANDARD FOR QUICK-FROZEN BLUEBERRIES CXS 103-1981 Adopted in 1981. Amended in 2019, 2022.

sections.section: ## STANDARD FOR QUICK-FROZEN BLUEBERRIES CXS 103-1981 Adopted in 1981. Amended in 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Title of the Standard for Quick‑Frozen Blueberries (CXS 103‑1981) showing adoption in 1981 and amendments in 2019 and 2022.
Notes that amendments were made in 2022 to Section 6.4 (Labelling of non‑retail containers) following decisions of the Forty‑fifth Session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.

Applies to quick‑frozen blueberries of specified Vaccinium species offered for direct consumption without further processing (except repacking), excludes products intended for further processing or other industrial purposes, and excludes bilberries which are covered by a separate standard.

Describes quick‑frozen blueberries (product and process), requiring preparation from specified fresh, clean, sound, ripe, stemmed berries of firm texture, packing with or without dry sugar or syrup, rapid freezing that passes the maximum crystallization range quickly and reaches −18 °C at the thermal centre, and handling/presentation provisions including free‑flowing or block style and recommendation to follow the Code of Practice for Quick‑Frozen Foods.

Defines quick‑frozen blueberries as product prepared from fresh, clean, sound, ripe and stemmed firm‑textured berries of Vaccinium corymbosum, V. angustifolium and V. ashei, packed with or without dry sugar or sugar syrup and frozen appropriately.

Specifies that freezing must pass the temperature range of maximum crystallization quickly and be complete only when the product temperature at the thermal centre reaches −18 °C after stabilization, and permits recognized repacking of quick‑frozen products under controlled conditions.

Specifies that freezing must pass the temperature range of maximum crystallization quickly and be complete only when the product temperature at the thermal centre reaches −18 °C after stabilization, and permits recognized repacking of quick‑frozen products under controlled conditions.

Quick-frozen blueberries must be handled during transportation, storage, distribution and up to final sale under conditions that maintain quality, and it is recommended to follow the Code of Practice for the Processing and Handling of Quick-Frozen Foods (CXC 8-1976).
Quick-frozen blueberries may be presented as free-flowing (individual berries not adhering) or non-free-flowing (a solid block).

This section covers essential composition and quality factors for quick-frozen blueberries including permitted sugars, composition limits for products prepared with dry sugars or syrup (soluble solids measured by refractometer at 20°C), definitions of 'defective' for composition, lot acceptance based on sampling plans with AQL 6.5, and quality criteria and tolerances (including analytical limits, free‑flowing characteristics, visual defect point system and sampling procedures).
Permitted optional ingredients for quick-frozen blueberries are sugars (sucrose, invert sugar, invert sugar syrup, dextrose, fructose, glucose syrup, dried glucose syrup).

Composition rules set soluble solids limits for blueberries prepared with dry sugars or with syrup (measured as sucrose by refractometer at 20°C), state that syrup must only cover berries and fill spaces, define a 'defective' sample unit as one outside those soluble solids ranges, and specify lot acceptance using an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5.

For blueberries prepared with dry sugars, total soluble solids of liquid extracted from a thawed, comminuted sample must be between 18% and 35% m/m expressed as sucrose, determined by refractometer at 20°C.

When prepared with syrup, only enough syrup to cover berries and fill spaces may be used, and the total soluble solids of liquid from a thawed, comminuted sample must be between 15% and 25% m/m expressed as sucrose, determined by refractometer at 20°C.
Any sample unit with soluble solids outside the ranges specified for dry-sugar or syrup preparations (Sections 3.2.1 and 3.2.2) is regarded as a 'defective'.
A lot is acceptable for compositional criteria when the number of 'defectives' (per Section 3.2.3) does not exceed the acceptance number (c) of an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5.

Quality factors require quick-frozen blueberries to meet varietal, colour, flavour and cleanliness criteria and set inspection/tolerance rules including a mineral impurity limit (≤0.04% m/m), a 10% m/m free‑flowing tolerance, definitions and a 300 g drained-berry sample for visual defect evaluation, with defects assessed by a point system and limits (including a 10% m/m limit for disintegrated/crushed berries).

The product must be of similar varietal characteristics, good colour, free from foreign flavour/odour, clean and sound and, with specified tolerances, practically free from sand/grit, stuck berries when free‑flowing, extraneous vegetable material, unripe berries, dissimilar edible berries, and reasonably free from cap stems, blemished and undeveloped/mummified berries.
Mineral impurities (sand, grit, silt) in the whole product (berries and packing medium if any) shall not exceed 0.04% m/m.

When labelled or presented as 'free-flowing', up to 10% m/m of berries that are stuck together and not easily separable in the frozen state is permitted, and the sample unit for assessing this is the entire container or as large a quantity as practicable.

Definitions provided: Extraneous vegetable material (EVM) means harmless leaves/plant portions; cap stems are stems ≥2 mm; unripe berries are completely green or predominantly green; dissimilar varieties are edible berries with distinctly different colour/shape/internal traits; blemished indicates insect, pathological or mechanical damage; undeveloped/mummified are badly shrivelled, dried or hard berries.
The standard sample size for evaluating visual defects and applying tolerances is 300 grammes of drained berries.

Visual defects for a 300 g drained-berry sample are assigned points per Table 1 (e.g., EVM, cap stems, unripe, dissimilar varieties, blemished [slight/material], undeveloped/mummified), with maximum allowable points (minor 15, major 8, total 15) and a 10% m/m limit for disintegrated, badly crushed or smashed berries.
Section heading only; substantive lot-acceptance provisions for quality factors are provided in the following subsections.

A sample unit is 'defective' for quality factors if it exceeds the mineral impurity tolerance (Section 3.3.2), exceeds the free‑flowing tolerance (Section 3.3.3), or exceeds the maximum total allowable points for visual defects or the disintegrated/crushed tolerance in Table 1 (Section 3.3.4.3).

A lot is acceptable for quality factors when the number of 'defectives' (per Section 3.4.1) does not exceed the acceptance number (c) of an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5, and each defective is assessed individually by characteristic.
No food additives are permitted in quick-frozen blueberries.

Sets hygiene recommendations for the product covered by this standard: preparation and handling should follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex codes; product should be free from objectionable matter to the extent possible in good manufacturing practice; and, when tested, must be free from microorganisms, parasites, or microbial-origin substances present in amounts that may hazard health.

sections.section: 5.1 It  is  recommended that  the product covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), 3  and other codes of practice recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission which are relevant to this product.
sections.summary: Recommends that the product covered by this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant Codex codes of practice.

sections.section: 5.2 To the extent possible in good manufacturing practice, the product shall be free from objectionable matter.
sections.summary: Requires that, to the extent possible under good manufacturing practice, the product be free from objectionable matter.

Adds product-specific labelling provisions to the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods: specifies naming and package-state requirements, retail storage/thawing information, special rules for bulk packs, and reference to non-retail container labelling standards.

Mandates that the label include the name "blueberries", indicate the packing medium as "with (name of sweetener and whether as such or as the syrup)", and state "quick frozen" (with the allowance that some countries may use "frozen" per Section 2.2 conditions).
Requires retail packs to include information on keeping (storage) and thawing of the product.

For quick-frozen blueberries in bulk, the required label information must appear on the container or in accompanying documents, but the food name with "quick frozen" (or "frozen" per Section 6.1.3) and the manufacturer/packer name and address must appear on the container.
States that labelling of non-retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Specifies packaging requirements for quick-frozen blueberries: packaging must protect organoleptic and other quality attributes, guard against microbiological and other contamination, prevent dehydration and leakage as far as technologically practicable, and must not impart odour, taste, colour or other foreign characteristics through processing and distribution.
Requires that methods of analysis and sampling for compliance with this standard be those in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the standard's provisions.
